exam.title: Google Cloud Professional Cloud Architect Renewal Exam – AI / ML Focus

sections.title: Designing and Planning a Cloud Solution Architecture
Managing and Provisioning a Solution Infrastructure
Analyzing and Optimizing Technical and Business Processes

questions.scenario: Cymbal Retail wants to add a generative AI-powered product description writer to their catalog management portal. Merchandising staff will enter a product name, category, and a few bullet points; the system must return a polished multi-paragraph description in under three seconds. The team has no ML expertise but needs to go to production within four weeks. Which approach best meets these requirements?

options.A: Fine-tune a text-bison model on Vertex AI using the existing product catalog as training data, deploy it to a Vertex AI Endpoint, and call the endpoint from the portal backend.
options.B: Call the Vertex AI Gemini API (gemini-pro) directly from the portal backend using a well-crafted prompt template that injects the product name, category, and bullets; no model training is required.
options.C: Train a custom seq2seq Transformer model on Cloud TPUs using the product catalog, export it to Cloud Storage, and serve it with TensorFlow Serving on GKE.
options.D: Deploy a self-hosted open-source LLM on a Compute Engine A2 GPU instance and expose it through an internal load balancer for the portal to call.

questions.explanation: Calling the Gemini API with a prompt template requires zero ML training, leverages a state-of-the-art foundation model, and can be integrated into any backend in hours — well within the four-week timeline. Prompt engineering to inject product metadata is a standard, low-effort pattern. Fine-tuning (A) adds weeks of data preparation and training complexity that is unnecessary when zero-shot or few-shot prompting of Gemini already produces high-quality marketing copy. Training a custom Transformer (C) would require ML expertise, months of work, and significant compute cost. Self-hosting an open-source LLM (D) requires infrastructure management, GPU provisioning, and operational overhead that conflicts with the team's limited ML expertise.

questions.scenario: Altostrat Media wants to build a real-time content moderation pipeline to flag potentially policy-violating video thumbnails uploaded by creators. The pipeline must evaluate every thumbnail within 500 milliseconds of upload and scale to 50,000 uploads per hour at peak. The team wants to avoid training and maintaining a custom vision model. Which architecture best meets these requirements?

options.A: Store thumbnails in Cloud Storage; run a nightly Vertex AI Batch Prediction job using an AutoML Image Classification model trained on labeled policy violations.
options.B: On each Cloud Storage upload event, trigger a Cloud Run service that calls the Cloud Vision API SafeSearch Detection endpoint to score the image for adult, violent, and racy content; write results to Firestore for downstream review workflows; use Cloud Run's automatic scaling to handle upload bursts.
options.C: Train a custom CNN on Vertex AI Custom Training using labeled thumbnail data, deploy to a Vertex AI Endpoint, and call it synchronously on each upload via an event-driven Cloud Function.
options.D: Use Dataflow to stream thumbnails through a Python pipeline that calls an on-premises moderation API over Cloud VPN for policy compliance.

questions.explanation: The Cloud Vision API SafeSearch Detection is a pretrained, fully managed service that responds in milliseconds, requires no model training or maintenance, and scales seamlessly with Cloud Run's per-request auto-scaling. This combination satisfies the 500 ms latency requirement and the 50,000 uploads/hour throughput without custom model development. Batch prediction (A) does not meet the real-time 500 ms requirement. A custom CNN (C) meets latency requirements but introduces substantial model training, labeling, and retraining overhead. Routing traffic to an on-premises API (D) introduces VPN latency, dependency on on-premises availability, and operational complexity.

questions.scenario: Cymbal Retail's data science team wants to build a customer churn prediction model using 18 months of purchase, browsing, and support-ticket history stored in BigQuery. The team is proficient in SQL but has limited Python and MLOps experience. They want the fastest path to a deployed prediction endpoint that their CRM system can call via REST. Which approach minimizes time-to-production while enabling iterative improvement?

options.A: Use BigQuery ML to create a LOGISTIC_REG or BOOSTED_TREE_CLASSIFIER model directly in BigQuery with CREATE MODEL; use ML.PREDICT for batch scoring and export the model to Vertex AI for online serving via the Vertex AI Model Registry.
options.B: Use Vertex AI AutoML Tabular to train a churn model on a BigQuery-connected dataset, deploy to a Vertex AI Endpoint, and call the REST endpoint from the CRM.
options.C: Write a scikit-learn pipeline in Vertex AI Workbench, containerize it, train with Vertex AI Custom Training, register in Vertex AI Model Registry, and deploy to a Vertex AI Endpoint.
options.D: Export BigQuery data to Cloud Storage as CSV, load it into a Vertex AI Managed Dataset, train with Vertex AI AutoML, and expose predictions via a Cloud Run proxy that transforms the CRM payload.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI AutoML Tabular handles feature engineering, model selection, hyperparameter tuning, and deployment with a no-code interface. The team connects directly to the existing BigQuery dataset, trains with a few clicks, and deploys to a managed Vertex AI Endpoint in hours. The REST endpoint integrates directly with the CRM. Iterative improvement is supported through model versioning in the Vertex AI Model Registry. BigQuery ML (A) is excellent for batch scoring but requires additional steps to serve online predictions; it is a valid approach but involves more configuration for a live REST endpoint. The scikit-learn custom pipeline (C) provides maximum flexibility but requires Python expertise and significant MLOps work, conflicting with the team's SQL-centric skill set. Option D duplicates the BigQuery-to-AutoML connection unnecessarily and adds a Cloud Run proxy layer without clear benefit.

questions.scenario: Altostrat Media wants to implement a semantic video search feature that lets viewers type natural-language queries such as 'tense penalty shoot-out moments' and retrieve relevant video clips. Their catalog contains 2 million short clips, each represented by a 768-dimensional embedding generated by a Vertex AI text-and-video multimodal model. Query latency must be under 200 ms at the 95th percentile with the full catalog. Which serving infrastructure should you recommend?

options.A: Store embeddings in Cloud Bigtable; at query time retrieve all rows and compute cosine similarity in a Cloud Run service using NumPy.
options.B: Store embeddings in AlloyDB for PostgreSQL using the pgvector extension; use an exact nearest-neighbor SQL query at search time.
options.C: Index the embeddings in Vertex AI Vector Search (formerly Matching Engine) using an approximate nearest-neighbor index; deploy a public or private endpoint; query the endpoint with the user query embedding at search time.
options.D: Store embeddings in BigQuery as ARRAY<FLOAT64> columns; use a BigQuery UNNEST and ORDER BY cosine similarity query to return the top results.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI Vector Search is purpose-built for billion-scale approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) search on dense embeddings. It uses a highly optimized ScaNN-based index that returns results in single-digit to tens of milliseconds, well within the 200 ms budget for 2 million vectors. It is fully managed and auto-scales. Bigtable with in-memory NumPy cosine similarity (A) requires fetching all 2 million rows, which is far too slow and compute-intensive. AlloyDB pgvector (B) supports exact nearest-neighbor search which is accurate but much slower than ANN at millions of vectors, potentially exceeding 200 ms. BigQuery (D) is an analytical engine; scanning and sorting 2 million embedding arrays per query is orders of magnitude too slow for interactive search latency.

questions.scenario: Cymbal Retail's ML platform team wants to standardize feature computation so that features used during model training are identical to features served at prediction time, eliminating training-serving skew. Features include 7-day rolling purchase count, customer lifetime value, and product affinity scores, all computed from BigQuery tables. Which Google Cloud service is specifically designed to solve this problem?

options.A: Create a Dataflow pipeline that writes features to Cloud Bigtable for low-latency online reads; use the same Dataflow logic in a batch pipeline to generate training data.
options.B: Use the Vertex AI Feature Store to define, compute, and serve features. Register the feature definitions once; use the Feature Store online serving layer for sub-millisecond prediction-time lookups and the batch serving API to export consistent feature snapshots for training.
options.C: Store features in a Cloud SQL PostgreSQL database; write a Cloud Function to recompute features on each BigQuery table update; read from the same Cloud SQL instance during both training and serving.
options.D: Materialize features into a BigQuery table using scheduled queries; join this table at training time and at prediction time from within the model serving code.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI Feature Store is the Google Cloud service explicitly designed to eliminate training-serving skew. Features are defined once, computed by pipelines that write to the Feature Store, and then accessed through a consistent API for both batch training exports and online sub-millisecond serving. This single source of truth guarantees that the same feature values and transformations are used in both training and prediction. A custom Bigtable + Dataflow solution (A) can replicate the pattern but requires building and maintaining the consistency logic manually. Cloud SQL (C) introduces a transactional database as a feature store, which adds schema management overhead and does not natively provide the point-in-time consistency guarantees needed for training snapshots. BigQuery scheduled queries (D) handle batch refresh but cannot serve online predictions with sub-millisecond latency required for real-time inference.

questions.scenario: Altostrat Media's ML engineering team runs complex multi-step model training workflows: data extraction from BigQuery, feature engineering on Dataflow, distributed training on Vertex AI, model evaluation, and conditional deployment to a Vertex AI Endpoint. They want to orchestrate these steps in a reproducible, versioned, and auditable pipeline that reuses components across different model projects. Which orchestration approach should you recommend?

options.A: Write a Bash script that calls gcloud CLI commands sequentially; trigger the script from a Cloud Scheduler job.
options.B: Use Cloud Composer (managed Apache Airflow) to define a DAG with operators for each pipeline step; store the DAG Python file in Cloud Storage.
options.C: Build the pipeline using the Kubeflow Pipelines SDK (KFP) and compile it to run on Vertex AI Pipelines; define reusable components as containerized KFP components stored in Artifact Registry; use Vertex ML Metadata to track lineage.
options.D: Implement the pipeline as a Cloud Workflows YAML file, calling each service's REST API; store execution state in Firestore.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI Pipelines (powered by Kubeflow Pipelines) is the purpose-built MLOps orchestration layer on Google Cloud. It provides reproducible, versioned, container-based pipeline components, native integration with Vertex AI services (training, evaluation, deployment), and automatic ML metadata and artifact lineage tracking through Vertex ML Metadata. Reusable KFP components stored in Artifact Registry enable component sharing across projects. A Bash script (A) is not reproducible, versioned, or auditable and fails when individual steps fail silently. Cloud Composer/Airflow (B) is a powerful general-purpose workflow orchestrator but lacks native ML metadata tracking and requires more infrastructure management than Vertex AI Pipelines. Cloud Workflows (D) is a general serverless orchestrator suitable for microservice choreography but does not provide ML-specific metadata tracking or the component reuse patterns native to KFP.

questions.scenario: Cymbal Retail is building a generative AI assistant for customer service agents. The assistant uses Gemini to generate suggested responses based on the customer's order history and current issue. A security audit requires that: (1) customer PII must never be sent to the Gemini API in plaintext, (2) all prompts and responses must be logged for compliance, and (3) the feature must only be accessible to authenticated agents. Which architecture satisfies all three requirements?

options.A: Call the Gemini API directly from the agent's browser using a hardcoded API key; log requests client-side to a Cloud Storage bucket.
options.B: Build a backend API (Cloud Run) that: de-identifies PII in the customer context using the Cloud Data Loss Prevention (DLP) API before constructing the prompt; calls the Gemini API using a service account with Vertex AI User IAM role; logs the de-identified prompt and response to Cloud Logging with a Log Sink to BigQuery; protects the API with Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) so only authenticated agents can access it.
options.C: Call the Gemini API from a Cloud Function triggered by the agent UI; pass raw customer data including PII; rely on Gemini's data handling policies for compliance.
options.D: Deploy a self-hosted open-source LLM on a Compute Engine instance to avoid sending data to any Google-managed API; expose it on the internal network.

questions.explanation: The Cloud Run backend acts as a secure proxy that satisfies all three requirements: DLP de-identification removes PII before the prompt reaches the Gemini API; Cloud Logging with a BigQuery sink provides immutable, queryable audit records of every interaction; and IAP enforces Google identity-based authentication, ensuring only authorized agents can invoke the endpoint. Calling the API from the browser (A) exposes API keys client-side and logs cannot be trusted. Passing raw PII to the Gemini API (C) violates the audit requirement. Self-hosting an LLM (D) avoids the Gemini API but introduces significant infrastructure, operational overhead, and does not inherently satisfy the logging or authentication requirements.

questions.scenario: Altostrat Media is deploying a Gemini-powered content tagging system that automatically reads uploaded video metadata and suggests genre, mood, and content rating tags. The legal team requires that the system must not generate content rating suggestions that could be challenged as biased against protected groups, and all model outputs must be explainable to regulators. Which combination of controls should you implement?

options.A: Use Gemini with default settings; add a disclaimer in the UI that tags are AI-generated.
options.B: Enable Vertex AI Model Monitoring on the deployed endpoint to detect distribution drift in input features; configure safety filters for the Gemini API to block harmful categories; use Vertex AI Explainability (feature attributions) on classification models; conduct periodic bias evaluations using held-out test sets segmented by protected attributes; document model cards in the Vertex AI Model Registry.
options.C: Add a post-processing Python script that randomly shuffles the top-3 tag suggestions to reduce perceived bias.
options.D: Switch to a rule-based system using regular expressions on the metadata text to eliminate any ML-related bias risk.

questions.explanation: A responsible AI architecture for a regulated use case requires layered controls: Gemini API safety filters reduce harmful output risk; Vertex AI Model Monitoring detects when input distributions shift in ways that could trigger biased outputs; Explainability features allow regulators to understand what drove a specific prediction; bias evaluations on protected-attribute segments provide evidence of fairness; and model cards document intended use, limitations, and evaluation results. These controls align with Google's Responsible AI practices. A disclaimer (A) acknowledges risk but controls nothing. Random shuffling (C) destroys ranking quality without reducing actual bias. Rule-based systems (D) avoid ML bias but cannot achieve the semantic richness needed for genre and mood tagging and introduce their own rule-authoring biases.

questions.scenario: Cymbal Retail's data science team spends 60% of their time preparing training datasets: joining tables in BigQuery, applying transformations, handling missing values, and exporting data to Cloud Storage for Vertex AI Custom Training jobs. Each new model experiment requires repeating this work. Which Google Cloud capability most directly reduces this data preparation burden while maintaining reproducibility?

options.A: Use Cloud Composer to schedule the BigQuery export jobs nightly so data is always fresh.
options.B: Use Vertex AI Managed Datasets with a BigQuery data source; define transformation logic as part of the Vertex AI Pipeline so that data preprocessing is versioned alongside the model training code and can be rerun reproducibly for each experiment.
options.C: Give data scientists direct BigQuery editor access so they can iterate faster on SQL transformations without bottlenecks.
options.D: Use Dataproc to run Spark SQL transformation jobs; write outputs to Cloud Storage for subsequent training steps.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI Managed Datasets with BigQuery data sources allow the team to register a dataset once and apply versioned transformation pipelines. By codifying preprocessing in a Vertex AI Pipeline component, each experiment automatically executes the same transformation logic, producing reproducible training data snapshots tracked in Vertex ML Metadata. This eliminates ad-hoc manual steps. Cloud Composer scheduling (A) automates the export but does not version transformations alongside experiments. Broader BigQuery access (C) speeds individual iteration but does not make preprocessing reproducible or reduce duplicated work across experiments. Dataproc Spark SQL (D) can handle complex transformations but adds infrastructure overhead and does not natively integrate transformation versioning with Vertex AI experiment tracking.

questions.scenario: Altostrat Media deploys a video recommendation model to a Vertex AI Endpoint. Three months after deployment, the business notices that click-through rates on recommendations have declined by 18%, despite no code changes. The data science team suspects the model's input feature distribution has shifted as viewer preferences evolved. Which Vertex AI capability should they enable immediately to detect and alert on this type of model degradation?

options.A: Enable Vertex AI Pipelines to retrain the model weekly on a fixed schedule.
options.B: Use Cloud Monitoring custom metrics to track the average prediction score returned by the endpoint over time.
options.C: Enable Vertex AI Model Monitoring on the endpoint to continuously compare the statistical distribution of live prediction requests against the training data baseline; configure drift detection alerts for features and prediction outputs to trigger a Pub/Sub notification when drift exceeds defined thresholds.
options.D: Create a BigQuery export of all prediction requests and run a weekly SQL query to compute mean and standard deviation of each feature.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI Model Monitoring is specifically designed to detect training-serving skew (difference between training data and live request distributions) and prediction drift (change in prediction distributions over time). It continuously samples live traffic, computes statistical distance metrics (Jensen-Shannon divergence, L-infinity norm), compares them to the training baseline, and fires alerts via Pub/Sub when thresholds are exceeded. This is the direct answer to detecting the model degradation described. Scheduled retraining (A) may address the root cause but does not detect or alert on drift — the team would still be flying blind about when and why quality degraded. A custom Cloud Monitoring metric (B) tracks average prediction score but is not sensitive to input feature distribution shifts that precede output quality degradation. Manual weekly BigQuery analysis (D) is a lagging indicator with no automated alerting.

questions.scenario: Cymbal Retail wants to build a customer-facing shopping assistant chatbot embedded in their mobile app and website. The assistant must answer questions about products, orders, and store policies by grounding responses in Cymbal's own product catalog and FAQ knowledge base — it must not hallucinate product details. The solution must support follow-up questions within a conversation session. Which architecture should you recommend?

options.A: Use Dialogflow CX with pre-built intents and static response templates stored in Firestore.
options.B: Use Vertex AI Agent Builder (Vertex AI Search and Conversation) to create a grounded generative AI agent: index the product catalog and FAQ documents in a Vertex AI Search data store; configure a Conversational Agent that uses Gemini as the reasoning engine with Vertex AI Search grounding to retrieve relevant product and policy content before generating each response; maintain conversation history in the agent's built-in session management.
options.C: Deploy a fine-tuned Gemini model on a Vertex AI Endpoint with the product catalog embedded in the system prompt at every request.
options.D: Train a custom BERT-based intent classifier on Vertex AI and return static answers from a lookup table in Cloud Spanner.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI Agent Builder (Vertex AI Search and Conversation) is purpose-built for grounded generative AI agents. Indexing the product catalog and FAQ in a Vertex AI Search data store enables Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG): the agent retrieves the most relevant documents for each user query and grounds the Gemini-generated response in that retrieved content, preventing hallucination of product details. Built-in session management supports multi-turn conversations natively. Dialogflow CX (A) is excellent for structured intent-based flows but does not natively support free-form generative answers grounded in a knowledge base. Embedding the full catalog in a system prompt (C) is not feasible for large catalogs (token limits) and does not update dynamically as the catalog changes. A BERT classifier with lookup tables (D) cannot handle the breadth of natural-language shopping questions and requires ongoing intent engineering as products evolve.

questions.scenario: Cymbal Retail's ML platform team wants to implement a CI/CD pipeline for machine learning models. When a data scientist merges a feature branch into main, the pipeline should automatically retrain the model, evaluate it against a holdout dataset, compare performance to the currently deployed model, and only promote the new model to the production Vertex AI Endpoint if it exceeds the current model's F1 score by at least 1%. Which combination of Google Cloud services implements this pattern correctly?

options.A: Use Cloud Build to trigger a shell script that runs the training job and manually updates the Vertex AI Endpoint.
options.B: Use Cloud Build to trigger on the git merge event; the Cloud Build pipeline invokes a Vertex AI Pipeline that contains: a training component, an evaluation component that writes metrics to Vertex ML Metadata, a model upload step to Vertex AI Model Registry, and a conditional deployment step that calls the Vertex AI Endpoint update API only if the new model's F1 exceeds the challenger threshold; use Cloud Deploy for staged rollout approval.
options.C: Configure a scheduled Vertex AI Pipeline to run nightly, retrain the model, and always deploy the latest trained model to the endpoint regardless of evaluation results.
options.D: Use GitHub Actions to SSH into a Vertex AI Workbench notebook and run retraining cells programmatically after each merge.

questions.explanation: This is the canonical CI/CD for ML (CI/CD4ML) pattern on Google Cloud. Cloud Build provides the SCM trigger and build runner; Vertex AI Pipelines provides reproducible, versioned ML workflow orchestration with native integration to Vertex AI services; Vertex ML Metadata stores evaluation metrics per pipeline run for auditability; the Vertex AI Model Registry maintains model versions and their evaluation metadata; the conditional deployment step implements the champion/challenger comparison; Cloud Deploy provides a gated promotion stage for production rollout. A Cloud Build shell script (A) lacks structured evaluation logic and auditability. Always deploying the latest model (C) without evaluation gates risks deploying a regressed model. GitHub Actions SSHing into a Workbench notebook (D) is fragile, not reproducible, and has no evaluation gating.

questions.scenario: Your organization needs to add a document intelligence capability to process thousands of insurance claim forms per day. Each form is a scanned PDF containing structured fields (policy number, claimant name, date of loss) as well as free-text damage descriptions. Extracted data must flow into BigQuery for downstream analytics within 30 minutes of form receipt. Which architecture should you recommend?

options.A: Use Cloud Vision API OCR to extract all text; write a custom Python parser to identify fields using regex; load results to BigQuery.
options.B: Use Document AI with a custom Form Parser processor trained on your insurance claim forms to extract both structured fields and free-text blocks; trigger extraction via a Cloud Run service on each Cloud Storage upload event; write structured output to BigQuery via the BigQuery Storage Write API for near-real-time availability.
options.C: Send all PDFs to a Vertex AI Gemini Vision endpoint with a prompt asking it to extract all fields as JSON; parse the response and insert into BigQuery using a Cloud Function.
options.D: Use Cloud Dataflow with a custom Python DoFn that calls Tesseract OCR for text extraction and rule-based NLP for field identification.

questions.explanation: Document AI Form Parser is specifically designed for structured document extraction. A processor trained on insurance claim forms learns the layout and field semantics, extracting both labeled key-value pairs and free-text regions with high accuracy. The event-driven Cloud Run trigger provides near-real-time processing, and the BigQuery Storage Write API ensures sub-second write latency. Custom regex parsers (A) are brittle and cannot handle layout variation across form versions. Gemini Vision (C) is a viable approach for prototyping but introduces non-determinism in JSON formatting and higher per-document latency and cost compared to a specialized Document AI processor. Custom Dataflow with Tesseract (D) requires building and maintaining OCR and NLP pipelines, replicating functionality that Document AI provides out of the box.

questions.scenario: Altostrat Media's data science team runs frequent Vertex AI Custom Training experiments using different hyperparameter configurations. After six months, they cannot reproduce results from earlier experiments because training code, dataset versions, hyperparameters, and resulting model metrics were tracked only in a shared spreadsheet that is now out of date. Which Vertex AI feature should they adopt to solve this problem?

options.A: Store all training scripts in a Git repository and tag each commit with the experiment date.
options.B: Use Vertex AI Experiments with Vertex ML Metadata: log each training run as an Experiment Run, recording hyperparameters, dataset artifact references, and evaluation metrics; use ML Metadata lineage to link input datasets, code versions, and output model artifacts; compare runs in the Vertex AI console to identify the best performing configuration.
options.C: Export model metrics to a BigQuery table after each training run and use Looker Studio to build a comparison dashboard.
options.D: Use Cloud Monitoring custom metrics to record loss and accuracy values during training for visualization in dashboards.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI Experiments with Vertex ML Metadata is the purpose-built solution for experiment tracking on Vertex AI. Each Experiment Run logs hyperparameters, dataset artifact URIs (enabling dataset version tracking), metrics, and code references in a structured, queryable metadata store. ML Metadata lineage automatically links input artifacts (datasets, code), execution (training job), and output artifacts (trained model), providing full reproducibility. The Vertex AI console enables side-by-side run comparison. Git tagging (A) tracks code but not hyperparameters, datasets, or metrics in a structured, queryable way. BigQuery export (C) captures metrics but not the full artifact lineage linking datasets and code versions to model outputs. Cloud Monitoring (D) provides real-time training metrics but not the structured experiment comparison and artifact lineage needed for reproducibility.

questions.scenario: Cymbal Retail wants to fine-tune a Gemini model to generate product descriptions that match their brand voice and terminology. They have 5,000 high-quality example pairs (product metadata input → brand-approved description output). Their ML team wants to fine-tune and deploy within two weeks using a fully managed service with no GPU cluster management. Which approach should they use?

options.A: Download the Gemini model weights, run QLoRA fine-tuning on a Compute Engine A3 VM cluster, and re-upload the adapted weights to Vertex AI.
options.B: Use Vertex AI Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) for Gemini: upload the training JSONL dataset to Cloud Storage, initiate a tuning job through the Vertex AI console or API specifying the base Gemini model and the training dataset, and deploy the resulting tuned model adapter to a Vertex AI Endpoint.
options.C: Use Vertex AI AutoML Text to train a text generation model on the example pairs.
options.D: Upload the 5,000 examples as few-shot examples in a system prompt and call the base Gemini API on every request without any fine-tuning.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) for Gemini is the fully managed, official path to customize Gemini with task-specific examples. The team provides a Cloud Storage JSONL file of input-output pairs, initiates the tuning job through Vertex AI, and the resulting tuned adapter is stored in the Vertex AI Model Registry and can be deployed to a Vertex AI Endpoint — all without managing GPU clusters or model weights. This meets the two-week timeline and no-cluster-management requirement. Manual QLoRA fine-tuning (A) requires GPU cluster setup, custom training code, and significant MLOps work. Vertex AI AutoML Text (C) is designed for classification, extraction, and sentiment tasks — not instruction-following text generation. Few-shot prompting (D) works for some tasks but 5,000 examples cannot fit in a context window and the quality of brand-voice adaptation is inferior to fine-tuning.

questions.scenario: Your organization is building a Vertex AI-based application that processes sensitive healthcare data to generate clinical summaries using Gemini. Regulatory requirements mandate that: patient data must not leave the EU, all API calls must be auditable, and the AI processing must occur within a VPC with no public internet egress. Which configuration satisfies these requirements?

options.A: Call the Vertex AI Gemini API over the public internet endpoint using HTTPS; rely on TLS encryption for data protection in transit.
options.B: Configure the Vertex AI API to use a specific EU region (e.g., europe-west4); enable Private Service Connect (PSC) to route Vertex AI API traffic through a private endpoint within your VPC, eliminating public internet egress; enable Cloud Audit Logs (Data Access logs) for the Vertex AI API to capture every API call with caller identity and request metadata; enforce region restrictions via an Organization Policy.
options.C: Deploy a self-hosted open-source LLM on a Compute Engine instance in the EU within your VPC to avoid using any Google-managed Vertex AI endpoint.
options.D: Use a VPN from your on-premises EU data center to call the global Vertex AI endpoint, treating the VPN tunnel as sufficient data residency control.

questions.explanation: Using a regional Vertex AI endpoint in europe-west4 ensures data processing stays within the EU. Private Service Connect routes Vertex AI API traffic over Google's private network fabric without traversing the public internet, satisfying the no-public-egress requirement. Cloud Audit Logs capture every Vertex AI API invocation with full caller metadata for regulatory auditability. An Organization Policy constraining allowed resource regions prevents accidental use of non-EU regions. Public HTTPS (A) violates the no-public-egress requirement. Self-hosting an LLM (C) avoids Vertex AI but introduces uncontrolled model quality, compliance certification gaps (the self-hosted model itself is not HIPAA/BAA covered), and significant operational overhead. A VPN to the global endpoint (D) does not guarantee EU data residency because the global endpoint may route processing to any region.

questions.scenario: Altostrat Media wants to automatically generate chapter titles and timestamps for their long-form sports video archive (100,000+ videos, each 1 to 4 hours long). The pipeline must process 500 new videos per day. Each video must be transcribed, divided into logical segments, and a descriptive title generated for each segment. Processing latency is not critical — same-day completion is acceptable. Which architecture should you recommend?

options.A: Process each video sequentially in a single Cloud Run service using the Speech-to-Text API for transcription and the Gemini API for segmentation and titling; run the service 24/7.
options.B: Use a Cloud Pub/Sub topic to queue new video events from Cloud Storage; trigger a Vertex AI Batch Prediction job for Speech-to-Text transcription; once transcription is complete, process transcript chunks through the Gemini API (batch mode or standard API) in a Dataflow pipeline to generate segment titles and timestamps; store results in BigQuery and update video metadata in Firestore.
options.C: Use Cloud Composer to orchestrate: a Cloud Transcoder API step for audio extraction, a manual review step requiring human annotators to create segments, and a BigQuery ML text generation step for titling.
options.D: Use the Video Intelligence API scene change detection to identify segments; use Cloud Natural Language API to generate segment titles from the scene metadata.

questions.explanation: This architecture correctly handles the batch nature of the workload. Pub/Sub decouples video ingestion from processing and provides durable queueing. Speech-to-Text API provides accurate transcription for sports commentary. Processing transcripts through the Gemini API (which understands context and sports terminology) generates high-quality segment titles and timestamps. Dataflow provides scalable, managed parallel processing of transcript chunks to handle 500 videos per day within the daily window. Results in BigQuery and Firestore enable downstream analytics and user-facing metadata updates. A sequential single Cloud Run service (A) would process one video at a time and likely fall behind the 500-video/day rate. Manual human annotators (C) defeat the automation goal and would not scale. The Video Intelligence API scene change detection (D) provides visual transitions, not semantically meaningful chapter boundaries for commentary-rich sports content.

questions.scenario: Cymbal Retail's ML team has deployed 12 separate Vertex AI Endpoints serving models for churn prediction, product recommendations, search ranking, price optimization, fraud detection, and more. Each endpoint is provisioned with dedicated compute nodes that sit largely idle during off-peak hours, driving a monthly Vertex AI serving cost of $120,000. How should you reduce serving costs while maintaining inference latency SLOs?

options.A: Delete the endpoints for lower-priority models (churn prediction, price optimization) and run them only on demand.
options.B: Consolidate compatible models onto Vertex AI Endpoints using model co-location where supported; migrate low-traffic models (churn, price optimization) to Vertex AI Batch Prediction for scheduled offline inference; for models requiring online serving, right-size compute nodes and enable autoscaling with minimum replicas set to zero for models with infrequent traffic patterns to enable scale-to-zero.
options.C: Move all 12 models to Cloud Run by containerizing each model with TensorFlow Serving; this provides scale-to-zero for all models.
options.D: Deploy all 12 models to a single large n2-standard-96 VM with a custom multi-model serving framework.

questions.explanation: The recommended cost optimization strategy combines multiple techniques: Vertex AI Batch Prediction eliminates always-on infrastructure for models that do not need real-time responses; autoscaling with scale-to-zero (where Vertex AI Endpoints support it, or via Vertex AI Prediction's minimum replica count of 0 for HTTP endpoints) eliminates idle compute during off-peak hours; right-sizing nodes to match actual traffic avoids over-provisioning. This multi-pronged approach addresses the root cause (always-on dedicated nodes) without eliminating capabilities. Deleting endpoints (A) reduces availability for production use cases. Cloud Run with TensorFlow Serving (C) can work for some models but requires significant containerization effort, may not support all model frameworks, and introduces latency overhead from container cold starts. A single large VM (D) creates a single point of failure and does not auto-scale, trading cost predictability for reliability risk.

questions.scenario: Altostrat Media is migrating their ML model serving infrastructure from self-managed TensorFlow Serving on GKE to Vertex AI Endpoints. They have 8 production models with different framework requirements: 3 TensorFlow SavedModels, 2 PyTorch models, 2 scikit-learn pipelines, and 1 custom XGBoost model. They need all models migrated within 6 weeks with no production downtime. Which migration strategy should you recommend?

options.A: Migrate all 8 models simultaneously over a single weekend maintenance window to minimize the migration period.
options.B: Use the Vertex AI Model Registry to upload each model with its appropriate serving container (pre-built TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn, XGBoost containers are available); deploy each new Vertex AI Endpoint in shadow mode (receiving a copy of live traffic) alongside the existing GKE endpoint; validate prediction parity and latency using Vertex AI Endpoint traffic splitting; gradually shift traffic from GKE to Vertex AI at 10%, 25%, 50%, 100%; decommission GKE endpoints after full traffic migration; execute one model at a time to limit blast radius.
options.C: Convert all models to TensorFlow SavedModel format before migrating to simplify the serving infrastructure.
options.D: Deploy all models to a single Vertex AI Endpoint using a custom multi-model Docker container that handles routing internally.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI natively supports pre-built serving containers for TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn, and XGBoost, eliminating framework conversion work. The shadow mode and traffic-splitting migration pattern is the zero-downtime standard: shadow traffic validates that the new endpoint produces equivalent predictions before any user traffic is shifted; gradual traffic splitting (10/25/50/100%) limits the blast radius if issues arise; one-model-at-a-time sequencing ensures failures in one migration do not impact other models. A simultaneous weekend migration (A) maximizes risk and provides no fallback if issues are found. Converting all models to TensorFlow (C) is unnecessary work and could introduce model quality regressions from framework conversion. A custom multi-model routing container (D) is operationally complex and eliminates the individual scaling and monitoring benefits of separate Vertex AI Endpoints.

questions.scenario: Cymbal Retail's fraud detection model, deployed on a Vertex AI Endpoint, has an SLO of 99.9% availability and p99 latency under 200 ms. Over the past month, the team has observed two incidents where Vertex AI Endpoint latency exceeded 500 ms for 10-15 minutes each, both triggered by traffic spikes during flash sales. What should you implement to prevent future SLO breaches?

options.A: Increase the minimum replica count to 0 so the endpoint scales down during quiet periods and has more room to scale up.
options.B: Configure Vertex AI Endpoint autoscaling with an appropriate target queries-per-second per replica based on load testing; set a minimum replica count sufficient to absorb flash-sale traffic spikes without cold-start delays; create a Cloud Monitoring alerting policy on the Vertex AI Endpoint latency metric (aiplatform.googleapis.com/prediction/latency) to notify the on-call team when p99 exceeds 150 ms; implement request rate limiting in the API gateway to protect the endpoint from unexpected traffic bursts that exceed modeled capacity.
options.C: Deploy the fraud detection model to Cloud Run instead of a Vertex AI Endpoint, as Cloud Run has faster cold-start times.
options.D: Add a Redis cache in front of the Vertex AI Endpoint to serve repeated identical fraud check requests from cache, reducing endpoint load.

questions.explanation: The latency SLO breach was caused by insufficient pre-warmed replicas during flash-sale traffic spikes. Setting a minimum replica count based on expected flash-sale traffic ensures the endpoint has warm replicas available before the spike, eliminating cold-start latency. Autoscaling configuration tuned to QPS-per-replica handles growth beyond the minimum. The Cloud Monitoring alert on endpoint latency provides early warning (at 150 ms) before the 200 ms SLO is breached, enabling proactive scaling or traffic management actions. Rate limiting prevents unexpected super-spikes from overwhelming even a well-scaled endpoint. Minimum replicas of 0 (A) is the opposite of what is needed — it causes cold starts, which is the root cause of the latency spikes. Cloud Run (C) may have faster cold starts for lightweight models but Vertex AI Endpoints provide better GPU support and native model serving optimizations for fraud detection models. Caching fraud checks (D) is generally inappropriate because fraud detection decisions must be made on the current transaction context, not cached results.

questions.scenario: Your company wants to build a generative AI application that answers employee questions by searching internal HR documents, IT policies, and engineering wikis stored across Google Drive, Confluence, and SharePoint. Answers must cite the specific document and section that grounded each response. The application must be ready for a pilot in 6 weeks. Which Google Cloud approach provides the fastest path to a grounded, citation-producing generative AI search application?

options.A: Export all documents to Cloud Storage as text files; build a custom RAG pipeline using Vertex AI Embeddings API to vectorize documents, store embeddings in Vertex AI Vector Search, retrieve top-k chunks at query time, and pass them to Gemini with a custom prompt.
options.B: Use Vertex AI Agent Builder (Vertex AI Search) with enterprise data store connectors for Google Drive, Confluence, and SharePoint; configure a Search App with grounding enabled; Vertex AI Search handles crawling, chunking, embedding, indexing, retrieval, and citation generation automatically; integrate the search widget or use the Discovery Engine API in your application.
options.C: Train a Gemini fine-tuned model on all internal documents so that it memorizes policy content and can answer from memory.
options.D: Use Cloud Natural Language API entity extraction on all documents to build a knowledge graph in Cloud Spanner; write a custom query engine to traverse the knowledge graph and formulate answers.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI Agent Builder (Vertex AI Search) with enterprise data connectors is the fastest path because it handles the entire RAG pipeline — crawling, chunking, embedding, indexing, retrieval, Gemini-powered answer generation, and citation extraction — without custom infrastructure. Native connectors for Google Drive, Confluence, and SharePoint eliminate data export steps. Citation generation is built in, satisfying the grounding requirement. The Discovery Engine API enables simple application integration within the 6-week window. A custom RAG pipeline (A) achieves the same outcome but requires weeks of infrastructure work: data pipeline, embedding jobs, Vector Search index management, and prompt engineering for citation formatting. Fine-tuning Gemini (C) on static document snapshots does not support dynamic document updates and cannot produce accurate citations to specific document sections. A knowledge graph (D) requires extensive engineering for entity extraction and query traversal and does not scale to unstructured policy documents.

questions.scenario: Altostrat Media's data science team works in Vertex AI Workbench notebooks for exploratory data analysis and model prototyping. The security team has identified that notebooks sometimes contain hardcoded service account keys and that large datasets are pulled directly from production BigQuery tables into notebook memory, violating data governance policies. Which architecture changes should you implement?

options.A: Restrict Workbench notebook access to the security team's review queue; require manual approval before any notebook code is executed.
options.B: Switch to Colab Enterprise (Vertex AI-managed Colab) with the following controls: attach notebooks to a user-managed service account with only necessary BigQuery read permissions using IAM; eliminate the need for service account keys entirely by using Workbench's built-in Application Default Credentials with the notebook's attached service account; enforce BigQuery authorized views or row-level security on sensitive tables so notebooks only access aggregated or anonymized data; enable VPC Service Controls to prevent data exfiltration from the Workbench/Colab environment; store no credentials in notebook files.
options.C: Rotate the compromised service account keys weekly and train data scientists not to hardcode credentials.
options.D: Move all data science work to on-premises Jupyter notebooks to avoid cloud data governance complexity.

questions.explanation: Colab Enterprise (or Vertex AI Workbench user-managed instances) with Application Default Credentials (ADC) using an attached service account eliminates the need for any keys in notebook code — ADC automatically uses the instance's service account identity. IAM least-privilege on the service account, combined with BigQuery authorized views or row-level security, enforces data governance at the data layer regardless of notebook code. VPC Service Controls create a security perimeter preventing data from being exfiltrated to personal accounts or external storage. This addresses both root causes: key management and raw production data access. Manual approval queues (A) create bottlenecks without addressing root causes. Rotating keys weekly (C) treats symptoms, not the root cause of hardcoded credentials, and does not address the raw data access policy violation. Moving to on-premises (D) eliminates cloud governance tools and does not solve either the key management or data access problem.

questions.scenario: Cymbal Retail's data team runs 50 BigQuery ML models for various business analytics tasks, including customer segmentation, demand forecasting, and return propensity scoring. The models are retrained by scheduled queries that run at various times, and the results are used by downstream dashboards and applications. The team has no visibility into which models are stale, which pipelines failed, or how upstream data quality issues propagate to model outputs. Which combination of tools provides end-to-end ML pipeline observability in this BigQuery-centric environment?

options.A: Enable BigQuery audit logs and send them to a Cloud Logging dashboard to track when scheduled queries run.
options.B: Use BigQuery Data Lineage (via Dataplex) to track data flow from source tables through BQML training and prediction jobs; enable Dataplex data quality tasks on input tables to detect quality issues before model training; configure Cloud Monitoring alerts on scheduled query failures; register BQML models in the Vertex AI Model Registry with evaluation metrics tagged at training time for staleness tracking.
options.C: Add a timestamp column to each BQML model output table and set a Cloud Scheduler job to check the timestamp daily.
options.D: Migrate all 50 BigQuery ML models to Vertex AI Custom Training so they can benefit from Vertex ML Metadata tracking.

questions.explanation: BigQuery Data Lineage through Dataplex provides automatic tracking of data flows: which source tables feed which BQML models, and which downstream tables consume model outputs. This makes it possible to identify how upstream data quality issues propagate. Dataplex data quality tasks validate input data before training runs, catching quality issues at the source. Cloud Monitoring alerts on scheduled query failures provide operational awareness. Vertex AI Model Registry integration enables staleness tracking by exposing model metadata alongside training timestamps and evaluation metrics. Audit logs (A) show when queries ran but not data lineage or quality propagation. A daily timestamp check (C) is a lagging indicator without root cause visibility. Migrating to Vertex AI Custom Training (D) would require significant rework and is unnecessary — the tools exist to provide observability in the existing BigQuery-centric environment.

questions.scenario: Altostrat Media's live sports commentary service uses a Vertex AI Endpoint serving a real-time audio-to-text transcription model. During a major tournament, the endpoint serving latency increased from 80 ms to 1,200 ms over 15 minutes as concurrent viewers spiked from 50,000 to 800,000. The on-call engineer had no warning before the SLO was breached. How should you redesign the observability and resilience of this endpoint?

options.A: Increase the Vertex AI Endpoint machine type to a4-highgpu-8g to handle any possible traffic spike.
options.B: Implement a multi-layer resilience and observability strategy: (1) Create Cloud Monitoring SLO objects on the Vertex AI Endpoint with a 99.9% latency SLO at 200 ms; configure burn-rate alerting to page the on-call team when the error budget is being consumed faster than expected — before the SLO is breached; (2) Configure Vertex AI Endpoint autoscaling with a target QPS per replica tuned to maintain sub-200 ms latency under load; set minimum replicas to handle expected baseline traffic; (3) Implement a circuit-breaker pattern in the application layer that falls back to a lighter cached transcription model or graceful degradation when latency exceeds 500 ms; (4) Load test the endpoint quarterly to validate autoscaling behaviour before major events.
options.C: Add a Pub/Sub queue between the audio stream and the Vertex AI Endpoint to buffer requests and prevent overload.
options.D: Switch to Vertex AI Batch Prediction for the transcription workload since it handles variable load better than online endpoints.

questions.explanation: The incident had two root causes: lack of early warning and insufficient autoscaling capacity. Burn-rate alerting on a Cloud Monitoring SLO object fires well before the SLO is breached (when error budget consumption rate indicates an impending breach), giving the on-call team time to act. Properly tuned autoscaling with adequate minimum replicas absorbs traffic growth before latency degrades. The circuit-breaker pattern provides graceful degradation when the endpoint is overwhelmed, maintaining a usable (if degraded) service rather than a broken one. Load testing validates the configuration before high-traffic events. Simply upgrading to a larger machine type (A) does not address the scaling pattern and may still be insufficient for 16x traffic spikes. Pub/Sub buffering (C) introduces queuing latency that is incompatible with real-time transcription. Batch Prediction (D) introduces significant latency (minutes to hours) and is entirely unsuitable for real-time commentary.

questions.scenario: Cymbal Retail wants to implement a multimodal AI feature for their mobile shopping app: customers can photograph a product they see in real life and the app returns visually similar products from the Cymbal catalog with price and availability. The catalog contains 2 million SKUs, each with 3–5 product images. The feature must return results in under 1 second. Which end-to-end architecture should you recommend?

options.A: Store product images in Cloud Storage; on each query, use the Vision API to extract labels from the query image and run a keyword search in Cloud Search against product descriptions indexed with those labels.
options.B: Use the Vertex AI Multimodal Embeddings model (multimodalembedding@001) to generate 1408-dimensional image embeddings for all catalog product images offline; store embeddings in Vertex AI Vector Search with product SKU metadata; at query time, generate an embedding for the customer's photo using the same model, query Vertex AI Vector Search for the nearest neighbors, and return the top matching SKUs with product details fetched from Cloud Spanner; cache frequent embeddings in Memorystore for Redis to reduce API latency.
options.C: Train a custom product similarity CNN on Vertex AI using catalog images with a triplet loss function; deploy to a Vertex AI Endpoint; perform exhaustive nearest-neighbor search in Cloud SQL against stored embeddings.
options.D: Use Google Cloud Retail API (Recommendations AI) product search feature with catalog images imported as product attributes.

questions.explanation: The Vertex AI Multimodal Embeddings model generates semantically rich image embeddings that capture visual similarity. Pre-computing embeddings for all 2 million catalog images offline (a one-time batch job) populates the Vector Search index. At query time, the customer photo is embedded and queried against the index using approximate nearest-neighbor search, which returns results in tens of milliseconds for 2 million vectors — well within the 1-second requirement. Cloud Spanner provides the low-latency product detail lookup for the matched SKUs. Redis caching of popular product embeddings reduces repeated embedding API calls. Keyword-based label search (A) does not capture visual similarity — a red Nike shoe and a red Adidas shoe share labels but may be very different products. A custom CNN (C) requires significant training data and compute; using exhaustive nearest-neighbor search in Cloud SQL does not scale to 2 million+ embeddings within 1 second. Google Cloud Retail API (D) is designed for click-stream-based collaborative filtering recommendations, not image similarity search.

questions.scenario: Cymbal Retail's customer service team wants to build an AI-powered agent that can answer complex, multi-step customer questions about order status, product availability, and return policies. The agent must search Cymbal's internal knowledge base (PDFs and HTML pages in Cloud Storage), perform real-time lookups against BigQuery order data, maintain conversation context across turns, and not hallucinate facts. Which approach best addresses these requirements?

options.A: Deploy a fine-tuned Gemini model on a Vertex AI Endpoint with a custom Python orchestration layer using Google Cloud client libraries to handle conversation state and data lookups.
options.B: Use Vertex AI Agent Builder to create a Reasoning Engine–backed agent grounded in a Vertex AI Search data store populated from Cloud Storage and BigQuery, with built-in tool use and multi-turn conversation management.
options.C: Implement a custom RAG system using Vertex AI Matching Engine for vector search, Cloud Spanner for conversation state, and a direct Gemini API call for generation, orchestrated by a Cloud Run microservice.
options.D: Use Dialogflow CX with a comprehensive FAQ intent library covering return policies and order status, integrated with Cloud Functions webhooks for real-time BigQuery lookups.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI Agent Builder (including Reasoning Engine) is purpose-built for grounded, multi-turn agents that can search a managed data store, use built-in tools, and avoid hallucination through RAG over enterprise content. Vertex AI Search data stores can be populated from Cloud Storage and connected to BigQuery, satisfying the knowledge base and real-time lookup requirements. Option A requires custom orchestration that duplicates capabilities already in Agent Builder. Option C is architecturally valid but significantly more complex to build and maintain. Dialogflow CX (D) is designed for scripted, intent-driven conversations, not the open-ended knowledge-base search described here.

questions.scenario: Altostrat Media stores content metadata (title, description, transcript excerpts, tags) in an AlloyDB for PostgreSQL database. The team wants to add semantic similarity search to recommend related videos to viewers using vector embeddings generated by a Vertex AI embedding model. Query latency must be under 50 ms at the p95 percentile, and the team wants to avoid adding a separate vector database. Which approach best meets these requirements?

options.A: Export all embeddings to a Cloud Storage bucket, load them into Vertex AI Vector Search (Matching Engine), and join the nearest-neighbor results with AlloyDB metadata at query time.
options.B: Store embeddings as a VECTOR column in AlloyDB for PostgreSQL using the pgvector extension, create an HNSW index on the embedding column, and run similarity queries using cosine distance (<=>)  in SQL.
options.C: Write embeddings to a Bigtable table keyed by content ID and perform approximate nearest-neighbor lookups using Bigtable key range scans.
options.D: Serialize all embeddings into a BigQuery ARRAY<FLOAT64> column and use BigQuery ML GENERATE_EMBEDDING for similarity search at query time.

questions.explanation: AlloyDB for PostgreSQL natively supports the pgvector extension, allowing vector embeddings to be stored as a VECTOR column alongside relational metadata. An HNSW index on the embedding column enables approximate nearest-neighbor search with sub-50 ms latency entirely within the existing AlloyDB database, requiring no separate vector store. Option A (Vertex AI Vector Search) would add operational complexity and an extra network hop for metadata joins. Bigtable (C) does not natively support vector similarity search. BigQuery (D) is not designed for sub-50 ms latency online queries, and GENERATE_EMBEDDING computes embeddings rather than searching them.

questions.scenario: Cymbal Retail is building a Gemini-powered product Q&A system. Before deploying to production, the ML team needs to systematically measure the quality of Gemini's responses across hundreds of test questions, evaluating groundedness (answers supported by provided product documents), coherence, and relevance. The process must be repeatable and trackable across prompt and model iterations. Which approach best supports this?

options.A: Manually review 20–30 randomly sampled Gemini responses before each release and document findings in a spreadsheet.
options.B: Use the Vertex AI Evaluation Service to define a pointwise evaluation task with groundedness, coherence, and relevance metrics; run the evaluation job against the test dataset; and log results to Vertex AI Experiments for tracking across iterations.
options.C: Write a custom Python script that calls the Gemini API for each test question and uses string-matching heuristics to score responses.
options.D: Deploy the Q&A system to a staging environment and use Cloud Monitoring custom metrics to capture user thumbs-up/thumbs-down feedback as a quality proxy.

questions.explanation: The Vertex AI Evaluation Service provides a managed framework for evaluating LLM outputs at scale, supporting both AutoSxS (side-by-side) and pointwise evaluation with model-based metrics including groundedness, coherence, relevance, and safety. Integration with Vertex AI Experiments enables systematic tracking of results across prompt versions and model iterations, creating a repeatable, auditable evaluation workflow. Manual review (A) is not scalable or repeatable. String-matching heuristics (C) cannot accurately measure semantic quality like groundedness. Production user feedback (D) is valuable but is not a substitute for pre-deployment quality evaluation.

questions.scenario: Altostrat Media's editorial team uses a Gemini-powered writing assistant to draft news articles and sports commentary. Editors are concerned that the assistant sometimes includes outdated statistics or factual claims about events that occurred after Gemini's training data cutoff. The team wants to minimize hallucination of current facts while keeping the assistant within their existing Vertex AI setup. Which solution best addresses this?

options.A: Switch from Gemini to a self-hosted open-source LLM that is retrained weekly on a current news corpus using Vertex AI Training jobs.
options.B: Enable Grounding with Google Search in the Vertex AI Gemini API call, allowing the model to retrieve current information from the web before generating a response and cite sources inline.
options.C: Implement a RAG pipeline using a Vertex AI Search data store populated by a daily batch job that ingests Altostrat's own archived articles.
options.D: Increase the model's temperature parameter and add 'Use only verified, current facts' as a system instruction.

questions.explanation: Gemini on Vertex AI supports Grounding with Google Search as a first-class feature: when enabled, the model retrieves fresh web content before responding, dramatically reducing hallucinations about recent events and providing inline citations. This is the lowest-effort, most direct solution—no retraining, no batch ingestion, no extra infrastructure. Weekly retraining (A) is operationally expensive and still lags real-time events. A RAG pipeline over archived articles (C) helps with Altostrat's own content but cannot provide access to current external facts such as live sports scores or breaking news. Adjusting temperature and adding instructions (D) does not give the model access to new information.

questions.scenario: Your organization wants to fine-tune a 70-billion-parameter open-source large language model available in Vertex AI Model Garden. The training job is expected to run approximately 72 hours on a dataset of 500,000 domain-specific documents. The team needs to minimize per-run cost while keeping total wall-clock time under 96 hours. Which compute selection best meets these requirements?

options.A: Use a single n1-standard-96 CPU-only Compute Engine VM; CPUs are cheaper per hour than accelerators.
options.B: Use a Cloud TPU v4 pod slice via Vertex AI Training; TPUs are optimized for large matrix multiplications in transformer architectures and deliver high throughput-per-dollar for LLM training at scale.
options.C: Use a single NVIDIA A100 (40 GB) GPU attached to a Vertex AI Training job; a single GPU is the most cost-effective option for any model size.
options.D: Use an NVIDIA T4 GPU-based instance because T4s have the lowest hourly cost among NVIDIA GPU options on Google Cloud.

questions.explanation: For 70B-parameter LLM fine-tuning, a TPU v4 pod slice provides the best combination of high aggregate memory bandwidth, inter-chip interconnect (ICI) for distributed training, and competitive cost-per-FLOP compared to equivalent GPU configurations. Transformer training is dominated by large matrix multiplications that TPUs are specifically designed to accelerate. A single A100 40 GB (C) lacks sufficient HBM memory for a 70B-parameter model without significant model parallelism complexity, and would be extremely slow for this job size. T4s (D) are entry-level inference GPUs; the wall-clock time for 70B-parameter training would far exceed 96 hours. CPU-only (A) is completely impractical for deep learning at this scale.

questions.scenario: Altostrat Media's content recommendation model predicts which videos a user is likely to watch next. The product team has received complaints that recommendations feel biased towards promoted content. The compliance team requires auditable evidence that the model itself (not post-model business rules) is not unfairly boosting promoted videos. Which Vertex AI capability best addresses the need for model interpretability and auditability?

options.A: Add a Cloud Logging sink that captures all prediction requests and responses for manual spot-checks by the compliance team.
options.B: Enable Vertex AI Explainability on the Vertex AI Endpoint; this provides per-prediction feature attributions (e.g., Integrated Gradients or Sampled Shapley) showing which input features most influenced each recommendation.
options.C: Re-train the model with L1 regularization to reduce the number of active features, making the model intrinsically simpler.
options.D: Deploy a BigQuery dashboard that tracks recommendation click-through rates segmented by content type to infer bias post-hoc.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI Explainability provides feature attribution methods (Integrated Gradients, Sampled Shapley, XRAI) that quantify the contribution of each input feature to a prediction at inference time. If the 'promoted content' feature has low attribution scores, the compliance team has auditable evidence that the model itself is not boosting promoted content. Cloud Logging (A) captures I/O but provides no insight into why the model made a decision. L1 regularization (C) reduces model complexity but does not provide per-prediction explanations. CTR dashboards (D) measure business outcomes, not model internals.

questions.scenario: Cymbal Retail's contact center handles 50,000 customer interactions per day across phone and chat channels. The team wants to deploy a virtual agent to automate common inquiries (order status, return policies, store hours) while seamlessly handing off complex issues to human agents with full conversation context. The solution must integrate with Cymbal's existing telephony system (SIP trunking) and CRM. Which Google Cloud service combination best meets these requirements?

options.A: Deploy a Gemini-powered Cloud Run service with a WebSocket API for real-time conversation, and build a custom SIP adapter using Asterisk on Compute Engine.
options.B: Use Contact Center AI (CCAI): deploy a Dialogflow CX virtual agent for conversation management, use CCAI Phone Gateway for telephony integration, and enable Agent Assist to surface real-time suggestions and conversation context to human agents during handoffs.
options.C: Build a Dialogflow ES chatbot for web chat only and use a third-party call center platform for phone interactions, synchronizing records via a nightly Cloud Composer job.
options.D: Use Vertex AI Agent Builder to build a knowledge-base chatbot deployed on Cloud Run, and direct phone calls to a human-only IVR system.

questions.explanation: Contact Center AI (CCAI) is Google Cloud's purpose-built solution for this scenario. Dialogflow CX provides enterprise-grade virtual agent conversation flows, CCAI Phone Gateway handles SIP-based telephony without custom Asterisk infrastructure, and Agent Assist delivers real-time AI suggestions and full conversation transcripts to human agents during handoffs—all with CRM integration via webhooks. Option A requires significant custom SIP engineering and provides no agent assist capability. Dialogflow ES (C) lacks CX's advanced flow control, and splitting channels adds complexity. Agent Builder (D) is suited for RAG/search agents and lacks telephony features.

questions.scenario: Altostrat Media's data scientists use personal Google Colab accounts for exploratory data analysis. Management is concerned that proprietary training data is being uploaded to personal accounts, notebooks are not version-controlled, and there is no audit trail of data access. The team wants a managed notebook environment that keeps data within the corporate Google Cloud project, enforces IAM-based access controls, and integrates natively with Cloud Storage and BigQuery. Which solution best addresses these requirements?

options.A: Require all data scientists to purchase Colab Pro+ subscriptions and use Google Drive sharing to manage notebook access.
options.B: Provision Vertex AI Workbench user-managed notebooks (JupyterLab VMs) for each data scientist so they get a dedicated VM they can configure freely.
options.C: Use Vertex AI Colab Enterprise, which provides a managed collaborative notebook experience running within the organization's Google Cloud project, enforces IAM policies, supports VPC Service Controls, integrates natively with BigQuery and Cloud Storage, and provides audit logs via Cloud Audit Logs.
options.D: Deploy JupyterHub on a GKE cluster with Google OAuth for authentication and store all notebooks in a shared Cloud Storage bucket.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI Colab Enterprise is specifically designed for enterprise notebook governance: notebooks run inside the org's Google Cloud project (no personal account data leakage), IAM controls who can create, view, and execute notebooks, VPC Service Controls prevent data exfiltration, Cloud Audit Logs tracks all notebook operations, and it integrates natively with BigQuery and Cloud Storage. Colab Pro+ (A) does not provide corporate project isolation or audit trails. User-managed Workbench notebooks (B) address some concerns but each scientist gets a full VM with direct internet access and no collaborative audit trail. JupyterHub on GKE (D) requires significant operational overhead and lacks native Google Cloud IAM and Cloud Audit Log integration.

questions.scenario: Cymbal Retail's supply chain team needs to forecast daily product demand 30 days ahead for 10,000 SKUs across 200 stores. The team consists primarily of data analysts proficient in SQL but with no Python or ML framework expertise. Forecasts will be consumed by a BigQuery-based replenishment system. Three years of daily sales data is already in BigQuery. Which approach best meets these requirements?

options.A: Export BigQuery data to Cloud Storage, use Vertex AI AutoML Tabular to train a forecasting model, and deploy it to a Vertex AI Endpoint for online predictions.
options.B: Use BigQuery ML with the ARIMA_PLUS model type, training directly in BigQuery via SQL CREATE MODEL statements with time_series_id_col for multi-SKU training, and generate batch forecasts using ML.FORECAST — all without leaving BigQuery.
options.C: Build a custom LSTM neural network using TensorFlow on Vertex AI Training, deploy it to Vertex AI Prediction, and orchestrate the prediction pipeline with Cloud Composer.
options.D: Use Cloud Spanner's time-series querying with a sliding window average to approximate demand forecasting without a dedicated ML model.

questions.explanation: BigQuery ML's ARIMA_PLUS model is purpose-built for time-series forecasting and can train across thousands of SKUs simultaneously using a single CREATE MODEL statement with the time_series_id_col parameter. Since training data is already in BigQuery and the team is SQL-native, this is the lowest-friction path—no data export, no Python, no new infrastructure. ML.FORECAST generates batch predictions stored directly in BigQuery for the replenishment system to consume. AutoML Tabular (A) requires data export and introduces a new interface, adding friction. Custom LSTM (C) requires ML expertise far beyond the team's capabilities. Cloud Spanner (D) is a transactional database, not a forecasting tool.

questions.scenario: Altostrat Media's content recommendation system uses Vertex AI Vector Search (formerly Matching Engine) to find the top-10 most similar videos for any given video based on 1536-dimensional embeddings. The corpus now contains 50 million video embeddings and is growing at 500,000 new embeddings per month. The team notices that ANN query latency is increasing and recall is degrading as the corpus grows. Which configuration change best addresses the scaling concern?

options.A: Switch from approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) to exact nearest-neighbor (brute-force) search to guarantee 100% recall as the corpus grows.
options.B: Tune the index's leafNodeEmbeddingCount and rebuild the index with a higher approximateNeighborsCount; also enable streaming updates to incrementally add new embeddings without full index rebuilds.
options.C: Move the embedding store to Cloud Bigtable and implement a custom ANN algorithm using range scans on a locality-sensitive hash (LSH) key schema.
options.D: Reduce embedding dimensionality from 1536 to 64 using PCA before inserting into Vector Search to reduce memory and search time.

questions.explanation: In Vertex AI Vector Search, index quality and latency are controlled by parameters: leafNodeEmbeddingCount controls tree granularity and approximateNeighborsCount controls the number of ANN candidates (higher values improve recall but increase latency). Enabling streaming updates (via the updateIndex API) allows incremental ingestion of new embeddings without a full index rebuild, eliminating the latency spike associated with monthly bulk rebuilds on a 50M+ corpus. Exact search (A) eliminates ANN scaling benefits entirely—O(N) complexity at 50M vectors is unacceptable for low-latency serving. Bigtable with custom LSH (C) requires significant custom engineering with no operational benefit. Aggressively reducing dimensionality to 64 (D) would severely degrade semantic quality.

questions.scenario: Your organization wants to fine-tune a 7-billion-parameter open-source language model from Vertex AI Model Garden to generate domain-specific legal contract summaries. Budget constraints limit training to $500 per run, and the team needs to iterate at least twice per week. Full fine-tuning of the 7B model exceeds this budget. Which fine-tuning technique best balances cost, training speed, and quality?

options.A: Full fine-tuning: re-train all 7 billion parameters on the legal contract dataset to maximize specialization.
options.B: Use Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA): freeze the base model weights and only train small rank-decomposition matrices inserted into attention layers, reducing trainable parameters by over 95% and dramatically cutting compute cost.
options.C: Use prompt engineering alone: craft detailed few-shot examples in the system prompt without any fine-tuning.
options.D: Distill the 7B model into a 500M-parameter student model first, then fully fine-tune the student model on legal contracts.

questions.explanation: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is a PEFT technique that inserts trainable low-rank matrices into transformer attention layers while keeping original model weights frozen. This reduces trainable parameters by 90–99%, cutting GPU memory requirements and training time proportionally—making the $500 budget and twice-weekly iteration constraints achievable. Vertex AI Model Garden supports supervised fine-tuning with LoRA for many open-source models. Full fine-tuning (A) requires updating all 7B parameters, far exceeding the budget. Prompt engineering alone (C) cannot match fine-tuning for highly specialized tasks like legal summarization. Knowledge distillation (D) is a multi-stage process that does not directly address the per-run budget constraint.

questions.scenario: Cymbal Retail's MLOps team runs Vertex AI Pipelines to train customer behavior models. The pipeline reads raw clickstream logs from Cloud Storage, processes them in Dataflow, and writes training datasets back to Cloud Storage before the training job. The security team has flagged that raw logs may contain PII (names, email addresses, payment card numbers embedded in URL parameters). How should the team address PII risk within this pipeline?

options.A: Restrict access to the raw-log Cloud Storage bucket to only the Dataflow service account using IAM; no data transformation is needed.
options.B: Integrate Cloud Data Loss Prevention (DLP) into the Dataflow pipeline as a transform step: inspect each record for PII using built-in infoTypes, and de-identify (redact or tokenize) PII fields before writing the processed dataset to the training data bucket.
options.C: Encrypt the training dataset with a customer-managed encryption key (CMEK) in Cloud KMS; CMEK encryption at rest protects PII.
options.D: After training completes, delete the raw log files from Cloud Storage to reduce PII exposure—PII risk only exists if data is retained long-term.

questions.explanation: The correct approach is to remove or transform PII before it enters the training dataset. DLP integrated as a Dataflow transform provides the most targeted solution: it inspects each record in the streaming/batch pipeline using built-in PII infoTypes (EMAIL_ADDRESS, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, PERSON_NAME, etc.) and applies de-identification transformations (redaction, tokenization, or pseudonymization) inline, producing a clean training dataset. IAM restriction (A) limits who can access data but does not remove PII from the data itself. CMEK encryption (C) protects data at rest but does not prevent PII from appearing in model training or leaking into model artifacts. Deleting raw logs after training (D) does not address PII that has already entered the training dataset.

questions.scenario: Altostrat Media runs nightly Vertex AI Batch Prediction jobs to score 200 million video-user pairs for the next day's recommendation cache. Each job currently takes 6 hours and costs approximately $800. The ML team wants to reduce cost without significantly extending the time beyond the 6-hour window. Which configuration changes best reduce cost?

options.A: Migrate from batch prediction to online prediction endpoints; online endpoints scale more efficiently for high-volume scoring.
options.B: Enable Spot (preemptible) VMs for the batch prediction job and increase the number of worker replicas to parallelize scoring more aggressively within the available time window.
options.C: Reduce the number of video-user pairs scored from 200 million to 20 million by applying a coarser pre-filter based on simple business rules.
options.D: Switch the model from a neural network to logistic regression to reduce per-record inference time and thereby lower cost.

questions.explanation: Vertex AI Batch Prediction supports Spot (preemptible) VMs, which cost 60–80% less than standard VMs with the trade-off of potential preemption.
